preamble: The transformation of the glottal fricative [h] into a glottal stop [ʔ] is a process of fortition or reinforcement. Instead of allowing air to flow through the glottis with friction, the vocal folds close completely. This can occur as a rhythmic reinforcement, especially in word-initial positions or under stress, and is sometimes a stage in the hardening of breathy vowels.

examples.note: Glottal reinforcement replacing initial aspiration in specific rhythmic contexts.

Proto-Mayan *h has multiple reflexes across the family. In several positions and branches it hardened to glottal stop [ʔ], while in other contexts it became [w], [j], [x], or zero. The fortition to [ʔ] is documented in word-medial positions in several Mayan languages and is one pathway by which Mayan languages developed their richly contrastive glottal stop inventories.

Attested in Yukatek and Chol dialects where inter-vocalic *h strengthened to ʔ. This process is related to the development of tone in Yukatek, where former vowel-length and *h/*ʔ contrasts merged into a high/falling tone distinction.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Ladefoged, P. & Johnson, K. (2015). A Course in Phonetics.
